items.myth: If I can solve problems on LeetCode, I'm interview-ready.
items.reality: Solo solving misses the hardest part: thinking aloud, handling clarifying questions, and adapting when the interviewer hints at a different approach. Mocks are necessary because they exercise those skills under pressure.
items.example:
```
Solo: 25-minute silent solve in your favorite IDE.
Live: 45 minutes narrating in a stripped editor with a stranger watching.
```

items.reality: Returns diminish after ~10–15 mocks if you don't act on feedback. Quality > quantity: one mock + targeted drilling beats five mocks with no follow-through.
items.example:
```
Week with 3 mocks + 0 drilling: marginal improvement.
Week with 1 mock + 4 days of targeted drilling on the top weakness: big improvement.
```

items.myth: AI mocks are as good as human mocks.
items.reality: AI mocks are great for warm-ups and 'I need a fresh problem now'. They're weaker at adversarial pushback, reading body language, and giving credible behavioural feedback. Use them as supplements, not replacements.
items.example:
```
AI mock: problem, hint, solution. Helpful but predictable.
Human mock: 'you seemed flustered when I asked about edge cases — try restarting that section'.
```

items.myth: Solving the problem matters more than how I solve it.
items.reality: For most companies, an unsolved problem with great communication and structured thinking beats a solved problem with silent, messy code. Interviewers grade signal, not outcome.
items.example:
```
Didn't finish, but: clear approach + clean partial code + correct complexity → 'hire'.
Finished, but: silent + spaghetti code + no edge cases → 'no hire'.
```
